timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Remnant Flees to Egypt

heb.paragraph: The people accuse Jeremiah: "You are lying (sheqer)! The LORD our God has not sent you" (v.2). The signature word of the book -- sheqer -- is now turned against the prophet himself. Those who lived by lies now accuse the truth-teller of lying. The irony is devastating: throughout the book, sheqer characterized false prophets, scribes, and the people; now it is hurled at the one person who never spoke it.

panels.ctx: The remnant rejects Jeremiah's counsel and accuses him and Baruch of a conspiracy to hand them over to Babylon. They force Jeremiah and Baruch to accompany them to Egypt, settling at Tahpanhes in the Nile Delta. The prophet who was commissioned as "a prophet to the nations" (1:5) now goes to the oldest of the nations -- not as missionary but as captive. The exodus is reversed: Israel returns to the house of bondage.

cross.note: The wilderness generation wanted to return to Egypt rather than enter the promised land -- the same pattern of preferring known bondage to unknown faith.
"Woe to those who go down to Egypt for help" -- Isaiah's parallel warning, now fulfilled.

notes.note: The accusation: Baruch is manipulating Jeremiah to hand the remnant to Babylon. The charge inverts reality: the one person consistently advocating for their survival is accused of plotting their death.

They take "all the remnant of Judah" to Egypt, including Jeremiah and Baruch. The prophet has no choice -- he is dragged into exile by the people he tried to save. They settle at Tahpanhes, a border fortress in the Delta.
Calvin notes the final irony: "They said 'We will obey whatever God says' (42:6), and when God speaks, they call it a lie. The speed of betrayal is breathtaking."
Tahpanhes (Greek: Daphnae; modern Tell Defenneh) was an Egyptian border fortress in the northeastern Delta. A significant Jewish community existed there, as attested by archaeological evidence.

Lundbom identifies this as "the final rejection of the prophetic word." The people who swore an oath of obedience (42:5-6) now accuse the prophet of lying. The covenant with the word is broken as thoroughly as the covenant with God.

Brueggemann reads the accusation as "the last act of interpretive violence." Unable to refute the message, they delegitimize the messenger. The pattern runs from Anathoth (11:21) through Pashhur (20:1) to this final dismissal.

heb.paragraph: God commands Jeremiah to bury large stones ('abanim) in the clay pavement at the entrance to Pharaoh's palace at Tahpanhes (v.9). The sign-act declares that Nebuchadnezzar will set his throne on these very stones -- Babylon will conquer Egypt itself. The people who fled to Egypt for safety will find the same enemy waiting for them there.

panels.ctx: Jeremiah's last recorded sign-act: burying stones where Nebuchadnezzar will place his throne. The message is absolute: Egypt is no refuge. Nebuchadnezzar did invade Egypt in 568-567 BC, confirming the prophecy. The final irony of the book's geography is complete: the people fled from Babylon to Egypt, and Babylon followed them.

cross.note: Ezekiel confirms that God gave Egypt to Nebuchadnezzar as compensation for his siege of Tyre.
The oracle against Egypt in chapter 46 provides the extended prophecy of Babylonian invasion.

notes.note: Burying stones is a foundation-laying act -- Jeremiah symbolically lays the foundation for Nebuchadnezzar's throne in the heart of Egypt. Babylon's authority will extend even here.
Nebuchadnezzar is again called "my servant" -- the third and final use of this title (also 25:9, 27:6). God's instrument of judgment operates across every national boundary.
Nebuchadnezzar will burn Egypt's temples and break the sacred pillars of Heliopolis (Beth Shemesh, "house of the sun"). Egypt's gods will be as powerless as Judah's idols.

Calvin develops the "my servant" theology one final time: "No empire is too great for God to use as his instrument. Egypt, which enslaved Israel, will now be enslaved by the same servant God used against Jerusalem."

The "sacred pillars of Beth Shemesh" are the obelisks of Heliopolis (modern Tell Hisn, near Cairo). The Egyptian temple of Ra at Heliopolis was one of the most important solar cult centers in the ancient world.

Lundbom reads the stone sign-act as "the final prophetic performance." From the linen belt (ch. 13) through the potter and flask and yoke and field, Jeremiah's sign-acts have progressively expanded in scope. This last one reaches to Egypt -- the prophet to the nations at work.
Brueggemann calls this "the universalization of judgment." Egypt -- the original enslaver, the perpetual alternative to God -- is not exempt from the same sovereignty that judged Jerusalem.

themes.note: Judgment and Sovereignty both at 9 -- Babylon as God's servant reaches even to Egypt. Mercy at 2: the time for mercy has been squandered.

lit.note: The reversed exodus reaches its conclusion: Israel returns to Egypt, and Babylon follows. The last sign-act declares that there is no escape from God's judgment.

hebtext.note: The people accuse Jeremiah: "You are lying (sheqer)! The LORD our God has not sent you" (v.2). The signature word of the book -- sheqer -- is now turned against the prophet himself. Those who lived by lies now accuse the truth-teller of lying. The irony is devastating: throughout the book, sheqer characterized false prophets, scribes, and the people; now it is hurled at the one person who never spoke it.

God commands Jeremiah to bury large stones ('abanim) in the clay pavement at the entrance to Pharaoh's palace at Tahpanhes (v.9). The sign-act declares that Nebuchadnezzar will set his throne on these very stones -- Babylon will conquer Egypt itself. The people who fled to Egypt for safety will find the same enemy waiting for them there.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Flight to Egypt

translations.text: They also led away all those whom Nebuzaradan commander of the imperial guard had left with Gedaliah son of Ahikam, the son of Shaphan—the men, the women, the children and the king’s daughters. And they took Jeremiah the prophet and Baruch son of Neriah along with them.

the men, the women, the children, the princesses, and every person whom Nebuzaradan the captain of the guard had left with Gedaliah the son of Ahikam, son of Shaphan; also Jeremiah the prophet and Baruch the son of Neriah.
So they entered Egypt in disobedience to the Lord and went as far as Tahpanhes.
And they came into the land of Egypt, for they did not obey the voice of the LORD. And they arrived at Tahpanhes.
